post: We toured George Washington's Mount Vernon home with the cast and crew of Young Washington  the estate where his ambition took shape. The film opens up a side of the founding father most people have never seen: not a distant myth, but a young man still becoming himself, a story each of us can recognize. Stand Together supported the film alongside Wonder Project, Angel Studios, George Washington's Mount Vernon, the Daniels Fund, and the Bill of Rights Institute. In theaters now.  #YoungWashington

The man who wrote the Constitution spent months arguing that the Bill of Rights was a bad idea. James Madison called written guarantees "parchment barriers." He thought a list of protected rights would leave every unlisted right exposed. Then, on June 8, 1789, he stood up in the First Congress and asked that body to pass one anyway. What changed his mind is the part most students never hear. It involved a promise he made on the campaign trail, a letter from a friend in Paris, and 2 states still refusing to ratify. Our first In Pursuit video is out today, built on an essay by Pulitzer Prize-winning historian Jack Rakove and produced with In Pursuit, an initiative of More Perfect. Free, under 4 minutes, with a companion lesson for grades 6-12. Watch:  https://bit.ly/3T9LrhO  #CivicEducation  #AmericanHistory  #BillOfRights  #SocialStudies  #TeachingResources

#TDiH 1798, the Sedition Act became law. The First Amendment was 7 years old. Federalists feared the Jeffersonian opposition would prove disloyal in the undeclared Quasi-War with France. Their answer: make it a federal crime to criticize the government. Every one of the 10 people convicted under the Act was a member of the opposing party. Matthew Lyon, a sitting congressman, was convicted for campaign speeches critical of Adams. He ran his reelection campaign from prison. He won. Madison and Jefferson didn't responded with constitutional argument, the Virginia and Kentucky Resolutions, and with elections. The Act expired in 1801. Jefferson won the presidency. He didn't renew it. One of the most teachable early tests of the First Amendment. Check out BRI's Primary Source Close Read 👇  https://bit.ly/4we1CZK  #TeachHistory  #CivicsEducation  #FirstAmendment  #ConstitutionalHistory  #AmericanHistory  #OTD

In May 1836, Congress passed a rule that automatically tabled, without debate, any petition mentioning slavery. When former President John Quincy Adams rose to protest, the Speaker of the House refused to recognize him. "Am I gagged?" he demanded. The answer was basically yes. And Adams spent the next 8 years fighting it, introducing 1000s of anti-slavery petitions from women, free black Americans, and enslaved people, surviving a censure attempt, and reading the Declaration of Independence into the record. Every petition was tabled without debate. On December 3, 1844, the House voted to repeal the gag rule. Even Adams's southern opponents acknowledged what he had done. Virginia Representative Henry Wise called him "the arch enemy of Southern slavery that ever existed." BRI's A250 Mini Documentary tells the full story. Watch it here:  https://bit.ly/4wFm3Pg  #USHistory  #HistoryTeachers

Most history and civics teachers aren't asking for much. A classroom set of Pocket Constitutions. A few books that bring history to life. Civics Champion is how that happens: teachers post what they need, and donors give directly to their classroom. Over 6,500 requests fulfilled and counting. Support a teacher today:  https://bit.ly/4coYN0s

At the Bill of Rights Institute, we believe citizenship doesn't begin at 18. The 2026 MyImpact Challenge proves it. This year, hundreds of students from 49 states and Washington, D. C. identified needs in their communities and took action through service, entrepreneurship, and volunteerism. Book drives. Business mentorship programs. Advocacy for people with invisible disabilities. A youth-led effort to bring digital literacy to seniors that has now reached more than 2,000 people in-person and over 3 million online. These are students who didn't wait for someone else to solve the problem. They saw a need, applied civic virtues like responsibility, humility, and justice, and built something real. Check out the 2026 MyImpact Challenge National Fair to meet this year's winners, including the sisters whose project took home the $10,000 Grand Prize. 👉  https://bit.ly/4fagPUD Know a student helping out their community? They can submit to the the 2027 contest starting September 11, 2026.  #MyImpactChallenge  #CivicsEducation  #StudentLeadership  #BillOfRightsInstitute

David Bobb, president of the Bill of Rights Institute, explains why respectful disagreement is an essential habit for civic education that's rarely in today's classrooms. He also offers his take for whether schools can recover it. 🏫 Read more:  https://edw.link/tk9

In 2025, BRI ran 315 professional development programs across 27 states and Washington, D. C., reaching over 8,000 teachers. The programs we're most proud of aren't the ones with the highest single-day turnout. They're the ones where we're still showing up years later. Our PD is built around the Founding documents  the Declaration, the Constitution, the Bill of Rights  and the civic principles that come up in every election, every courtroom, and every classroom debate. We design programming to compound: each session builds on the last, and each year gives teachers a deeper foundation to teach from. Fall planning happens in July. Constitution Day is September 17. What would your district's civics program look like with a PD partner for the next 3 years, not just the next 3 weeks? 🔗 Request a program:  https://bit.ly/4fZoZ46  #CivicsEducation  #ProfessionalDevelopment  #AmericanHistory  #K12Education  #ConstitutionDay

How does an ordinary young man become an extraordinary leader? That's the question at the heart of Young Washington, in theaters now, and it's the one director Jon Erwin digs into on the Going Big! podcast. His answer runs counter to how we usually teach the Founders: Washington wasn't forged in success. He was forged in failure. As Erwin puts it, success is where you cash in, but failure is where you learn. It's a compelling listen, and a preview of why this story belongs in classrooms. BRI has built family discussion guides and curriculum resources to help students dig into exactly these questions. Scan the code for the family guide. Full interview below 👇 Thank you to Daniels Fund for making these resources possible.  https://bit.ly/4buMJKj  #CivicsEducation  #Leadership  #AmericanHistory

Happy Independence Day 🇺🇸 250 years ago today, the Founders declared something that has never sustained itself automatically: Free and equal people can govern themselves in the pursuit of happiness. Every generation must learn it, question it, and choose whether to carry it forward. The next chapter of the American story is already being written in classrooms across the country. We're grateful for every teacher and supporter making that possible. Happy 4th of July from BRI!  #IndependenceDay  #America250  #CivicEducation  #July4th  #DeclarationOfIndependence
